---
title: "Module 7: Some Claude Tips"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## What We've Done

- AI + code tool
  - financial analysis (mean-variance, dcf)
  - deliverables (figures, excel, word, powerpoint)
  - small web apps (artifacts, pyodide)
- Skills (reusable instructions)

## Where We're Going

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**M7: Some Tips (Today)**]{.card-title}

- Use AI to critique AI
- Enhance AI memory
- Boris Cherny's tips
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M8: Web Scraping with AI**]{.card-title}

- Scraping SEC EDGAR, news, transcripts
- Static vs. dynamic pages
- Magnolia example
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M9: Build & Deploy Agents**]{.card-title}

- The API: software connections to LLMs 
- Chatbots and web apps
- Connecting tools
- FastAPI and deployment
:::
:::

## Where We're Going (cont.)

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**M10: Corporate Deployment**]{.card-title}

- Data security: on-prem or cloud
- Guardrails and governance
- ERP and AI disruption
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M11: Retrieval Augmented Generation**]{.card-title}

- How RAG works
- Chunking, embedding, and vector search
- Example: RAG for security analysis and valuation

:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M12: AI Sentiment Analysis**]{.card-title}

- LLMs as sentiment classifiers
- News and earnings call analysis
- Trading signals from text
:::
:::

## Using AI to Evaluate AI {.section-divider}

## Four Approaches

AI output looks polished, but polished is not the same as correct. Use AI itself to [stress-test]{.amber} AI work.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Single Reviewer**]{.card-title}

- **Use a different model** --- paste the output into a competing LLM and ask it to find errors
- **Use the same model** --- start a second conversation with the same model or use a subagent and ask it for a critique
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Multiple Reviewers**]{.card-title}

- **Use multiple subagents** --- spawn parallel reviewers with different lenses (correctness, clarity, devil's advocate)
- **Evaluate in a loop** --- have the agent produce, critique, revise, and re-critique iteratively until stable
:::
:::

[The more consequential the decision, the more reviewers you want.]{.amber}

## The /critique Skill

The `/critique` skill automates the multi-reviewer approach. It spawns [three parallel agents]{.amber} --- correctness, clarity, and devil's advocate --- then synthesizes and prioritizes the findings.

:::{.three-col}
::::{.column}
**Example**

I asked Claude to write a 5-page doc on the history of the World Wide Web.

[History-of-the-World-Wide-Web.docx](/files/History-of-the-World-Wide-Web.docx){target="_blank"}

::::

::::{.column}
**Results**

The critique found:

- [7 critical issues]{.amber} (wrong dates, understated stats)
- 8 warnings
- 7 suggestions

[View critique (markdown)](/files/History-of-the-World-Wide-Web-critique.md){target="_blank"}

::::

::::{.column}
**Try it yourself**

Download the [critique skill](/files/critique-skill.zip){target="_blank"} and ask Claude to install it.

::::
:::

[Caveat: AI reviewers share the same training data, so they can have shared blind spots. Use human judgment for high-stakes decisions.]{.amber}

## Enhance AI Memory {.section-divider}

## Saving Conversation Summaries

AI conversations are [stateless]{.amber} --- each new session starts from scratch. You can fix this by saving things to [markdown (.md) files]{.amber}.

**A summarize chat skill**

1. Download [summarize-chat skill](/files/summarize-chat-skill.zip){target="_blank"} and ask Claude to install it.
2. At the end of a chat, type `/summarize-chat`
3. The summary is saved as a `.md` file in a `chats/` folder.  Each session, Claude reads the most recent summaries on startup.

**Standard memory devices**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project-level instructions loaded every session
- **Skills (.md)** --- reusable task-specific instructions
- **Other files** --- you can always ask Claude to save any chat or part of a chat as a file

## Markdown Quick Reference

Markdown is a plain-text format that uses simple symbols for formatting. AI reads and writes it natively.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Syntax**

```markdown
# Heading
## Subheading

- Bullet point
- **Bold text**
- *Italic text*

1. Numbered list
2. Second item

> Blockquote

`inline code`
```
::::

::::{.column}
**Where you'll see it**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project instructions
- **SKILL.md** --- skill definitions
- **Session summaries** --- conversation logs
- **README.md** --- any GitHub repo

You can read any markdown file in Notepad, but you get a better presentation with a markdown renderer, for example, [markdownlivepreview.com](https://markdownlivepreview.com){target="_blank"}.
::::
:::

## Claude Dispatch {.section-divider}

## Delegate Tasks from Your Phone

Stuck in a meeting and realize a data pull needs fixing? Claude **Dispatch** lets you send a task from the Claude mobile app and have it [run on your desktop]{.amber} --- as either a Cowork or Code session, depending on the task.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Setup**

1. [Update both apps first:]{.amber} Claude Desktop (Help → Check for Updates) and Claude mobile app (App Store / Google Play)
2. Open **Claude Desktop** → **Cowork** tab
3. Click **Dispatch** in the sidebar
4. Scan the QR code from your phone
5. Send a task --- e.g., *"fix the auth bug"*

Dispatch decides whether the task needs a Code session (dev work) or can be handled in Cowork (research, writing).
::::

::::{.column}
**How it works**

- Your desktop must be **awake and running** Claude Desktop
- Dev tasks → Code session spawned automatically
- You get a **push notification** when done or when approval is needed
- Full access to local files, MCP servers, and project config

[Requires Pro or Max plan.]{.amber}
::::
:::

## Boris Cherny's Tips {.section-divider}

Boris Cherny is the **creator and Head of Claude Code** at Anthropic. In a series of X posts over several weeks, he offered tips on how he and his group use Claude Code.

The tips were compiled by a fan and made available online [here](https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/){target="_blank"} --- dozens of tips organized into 10 parts covering workflows, memory, automation, and more.

**A few highlights:**

- Run multiple agents in [parallel using worktrees]{.amber} to get more done faster
- Use [hooks]{.amber} to auto-verify work (e.g., run tests after every code change)
- Write detailed [CLAUDE.md]{.amber} files --- the better the instructions, the less you need to repeat yourself

His tips can be **installed as a Claude Code skill** --- paste the URL in Claude Code and ask it to create a skill from the content.

## Claude Plugins {.section-divider}

## What Are Plugins?

A **plugin** bundles multiple Claude Code components into a [single installable package]{.amber}:

| Component | What it does |
|---|---|
| Skills | Slash commands & auto-triggered prompts |
| Agents | Custom sub-agent definitions |
| Hooks | Event handlers (e.g., lint on file write) |
| MCP servers | External tool integrations |
| LSP servers | Language intelligence (type checking, etc.) |
| Output styles | Custom formatting for agent output |

## Installing and Sharing Plugins

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Installing**

- `/plugin install` from a marketplace or git repo
- Commands are [namespaced]{.amber}: `/my-plugin:hello`
- Versioned via `plugin.json` manifest

::::

::::{.column}
**Why plugins?**

- **Skills** are great for personal, single-project use
- **Plugins** are designed for [team distribution]{.amber} and cross-project reuse
- Available from the official marketplace, team marketplaces, or any git repo
- Browse community plugins: [anthropics/claude-plugins-official](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/tree/main/external_plugins){target="_blank"}
::::
:::

Skills are personal; plugins scale them to teams. Why does that matter for enterprise software?

## When 1 License Replaces 5

In early 2026, a wave of AI agent and plugin announcements --- from Anthropic, OpenAI, and others --- triggered a [historic selloff]{.amber} in enterprise software stocks. Wall Street dubbed it the [SaaSpocalypse]{.amber}. The thesis: if AI agents do the work of 5 employees, companies need [1 license instead of 5]{.amber}.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**The damage (as of March 2026)**

- [~$285B]{.amber} in software market cap erased in 48 hours (Feb 3--5)
- Goldman Sachs software index fell 6% in a single day
- IGV (software ETF) down [~25% YTD]{.amber}
- Forward P/E multiples compressed from ~34x to ~22x
- Software traded at a [discount to the S&P 500]{.amber} on a forward P/E basis --- a first

::::

::::{.column}
**Notable drops**

| Company | Decline | Period |
|---|---|---|
| Monday.com | -77% | from 52-wk high |
| Atlassian | -73% | from 52-wk high |
| ServiceNow | -49% | from 52-wk high |
| Salesforce | -43% | from 52-wk high |
| SAP | -16% | single day (Jan 29) |
| Thomson Reuters | -16% | single day (Feb 4) |
::::
:::

**The counterargument:** per-seat pricing may give way to usage-based or outcome-based models, but total enterprise software spend could still grow as AI capabilities expand.

[Sources: Bloomberg, SaaStr, Goldman Sachs; data as of mid-March 2026.]{style="font-size: 0.7em; color: gray;"}
